claims.claim_text: The Ericsson December 2018 outage paragraph attributes a multi-country service disruption to an expired control-plane certificate and adds specific impact and penalty figures.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Consider the highly publicized outage of Ericsson in December 2018. During a critical operational window, their primary infrastructure nodes went entirely dark, causing millions of cellular network users across multiple countries to lose service simultaneously. The core scheduling and routing mechanisms failed to deploy necessary workloads, and the engineering teams lost critical diagnostic access. The root cause? A fundamental control plane certificate had quietly expired. This single failure caused immense reputational damage and resulted in hundreds of millions of dollars in SLA penalties over the course of a massive day-long recovery window.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Certificate expiration can trigger large, public outages in complex distributed systems, so operators need documented renewal procedures and dependable node-level fallback diagnostics.
claims.rationale: This mixes a real telecom outage with unsupported Kubernetes-specific, financial, and impact specifics that are not safely attributable as written.

claims.claim_text: The module claims the same outage could have been limited to fifteen minutes and entirely mitigated by better static-pod observability and kubeadm certificate renewal knowledge.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: If the infrastructure engineers had maintained rigorous observability over their static pod manifests and had known how to rapidly bypass the defunct API gateway to manually verify static pod logs using `crictl`, the initial downtime could have been restricted to fifteen minutes. Furthermore, understanding how to immediately renew internal certificates via `kubeadm` would have mitigated the cascading failure entirely. Mastery of control plane troubleshooting separates novice operators from true Kubernetes experts who can confidently navigate a total system blackout.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When certificate expiry or API unavailability is suspected, operators should fall back to node-level tooling such as `crictl`, inspect certificate expiry, and rehearse renewal procedures before an incident happens.
claims.rationale: This is a speculative counterfactual with uncitable outcome claims.

claims.claim_text: The current stable Kubernetes version is 1.35, with an EOL scheduled for February 2027.
claims.anchor_text: The current stable Kubernetes version is 1.35, with an EOL scheduled for February 2027.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kubernetes 1.35 remains a supported release line through February 28, 2027, but it is not the current stable release as of May 7, 2026.
claims.rationale: This time-sensitive statement is outdated on the research date.

claims.claim_text: Control plane client certificates generated by kubeadm default to 1 year, while CA certificates default to 10 years.
claims.rationale: The shared kubeadm certificate source documents these default validity periods.

claims.claim_text: The kube-apiserver defaults to TCP 6443, and etcd uses TCP 2379 for client traffic and 2380 for peer traffic.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes ports reference lists these default control-plane ports.

claims.claim_text: The v1 ComponentStatus API used by `kubectl get cs` was deprecated in Kubernetes v1.19+.
claims.rationale: The API reference explicitly marks ComponentStatus as deprecated in v1.19+.

claims.claim_text: Requests to deprecated Kubernetes REST APIs return warnings starting in v1.19, which is why `kubectl get cs` warns on modern clusters.
claims.rationale: The deprecation policy documents warning behavior for deprecated API requests beginning in v1.19.

claims.claim_text: etcd defaults to a 2 GB backend quota and the module describes quota exhaustion as making the cluster read-only.
claims.anchor_text: A single etcd database cluster defaults to a maximum storage space quota of 2 gigabytes; if this limit is breached without compaction, the entire cluster enters a read-only state.
claims.suggested_rewrite: etcd enforces a backend space quota, and exceeding it can halt new writes until you free space and perform maintenance.
claims.rationale: The underlying limit is real, but the module's remediation and 'read-only state' wording is too imprecise for a hard factual claim.

claims.claim_text: The cloud-controller-manager was split from kube-controller-manager so cloud providers can release integrations independently from core Kubernetes.
claims.rationale: The cloud-controller-manager concept page states this architectural separation and motivation.

claims.claim_text: In kubeadm-based clusters, core control plane components run as static Pods from `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/`.
claims.rationale: The shared kubeadm implementation source covers the static-pod layout and manifest path.

claims.claim_text: When the API server is unavailable, `crictl` can inspect pods, containers, images, and logs directly through the CRI.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.2 Diagnosing Issues at the Node Level, Step 1 intro
claims.rationale: The shared crictl troubleshooting source directly supports this node-level inspection workflow.

claims.claim_text: kubeadm-managed control plane certificates do not automatically renew unless you run a cluster upgrade or renew them manually.
claims.span_hint: section: 2.2 Diagnosing Issues at the Node Level, Step 3 paragraph
claims.rationale: The shared kubeadm certificate source explains renewal behavior and upgrade-time renewal.

claims.claim_text: In HA control planes, kube-scheduler uses Lease objects in the `kube-system` namespace for leader election.
claims.rationale: The kube-scheduler reference documents Lease-based leader election and the default resource namespace.

claims.claim_text: etcd uses Raft and needs a quorum of `(N/2 + 1)` healthy members to continue functioning.
claims.rationale: The shared etcd operations source covers odd-member guidance and quorum implications.

claims.claim_text: As of etcd v3.6, etcd natively exposes `/livez` and `/readyz` HTTP health endpoints.
claims.span_hint: section: 5.2 Diagnosing Quorum Health, paragraph after duplicate command
claims.rationale: This version-specific etcd feature is documented on etcd.io, which is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The restore workflow paragraph says newer etcd releases use `etcdutl snapshot restore` instead of `etcdctl snapshot restore`, while also tying that change to Kubernetes 1.35 in an unsupported way.
claims.span_hint: section: 5.3 Backup and Restore Procedures, war story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: However, in etcd v3.6.0 (standard in Kubernetes v1.35 clusters), this command was completely removed. You must now use the `etcdutl snapshot restore` command.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In newer etcd releases, snapshot restore workflows may require `etcdutl` instead of `etcdctl`, so verify the exact command against the etcd version you are operating.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is real, but the Kubernetes-version linkage is not safely supported as written.

claims.claim_text: Static Pods are visible through mirror Pods in the API, but they are controlled from the node, so API edits do not replace the manifest-on-disk source of truth.
claims.span_hint: section: 6.1 How Static Pods Work, pause-and-predict paragraph
claims.rationale: The static Pod documentation states that these Pods are visible via mirror Pods but cannot be controlled from the API server.

further_reading.title: Troubleshooting Clusters
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers practical control-plane and node-level debugging flow when core components are unhealthy.

further_reading.title: kubeadm Implementation Details
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how kubeadm lays out static control-plane Pods and related host paths.

further_reading.title: Certificate Management with kubeadm
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly supports certificate expiry inspection, renewal, and default validity behavior for control-plane troubleshooting.

notes: Quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, drills, code blocks, and diagrams were intentionally excluded per the seed rules. Release-status facts were checked against current Kubernetes docs on 2026-05-07, which is why the 1.35 'current stable' wording was marked for rewrite.
